meta.title: APM Foundations: 21-Day PM Competency Roadmap
meta.framework: Reforge 12 PM Competency Model by Ravi Mehta

preReadingBeforeDay1.title: Behind Every Great Product — Marty Cagan, SVPG
preReadingBeforeDay1.why: 10-minute read that answers 'why does the PM role exist at all?' — having this context makes Day 1 framework reading land much harder.

days.theme: What the PM role actually is
days.objective: Map all 12 Reforge PM competencies to real PM responsibilities and explain — in writing — what an APM at Swiggy does differently from the engineering lead, the designer, and the data analyst on the same squad.

realWorldAnchor.example: Swiggy operates with dedicated product squads — one each for the consumer app, restaurant partner tools, logistics, and growth — where APMs are embedded in a single squad and own specific feature areas end-to-end alongside engineers, designers, and analysts.

days.teamContext: Today's topic touches all four cross-functional partners — Engineering (who builds), Design (who shapes UX), Data/Analytics (who measures), and Marketing/Growth (who acquires users) — because understanding the PM role requires seeing clearly how it differs from each of these functions.

tasks.title: Product Manager Skills by Seniority Level — Ravi Mehta, Reforge
tasks.summary: Ravi Mehta, former VP of Product at Tinder and author of the Reforge PM competency model, maps the 12 core PM competencies across three seniority levels — APM, PM, and Senior PM. This is the foundational framework for the entire 21-day roadmap; reading it on Day 1 gives Pranav a mental map of every skill he will build over the next three weeks.
keyTakeaways: The 12 competencies are organised into four pillars — Product Execution, Customer Insight, Product Strategy, and Influencing People — and each pillar matters at the APM level, though execution and customer insight are weighted more heavily than strategy and influence early in a PM career
APMs who outperform in Product Execution and Customer Insight get promoted; those who lead with strategy before building execution credibility often lose team trust first
Being 'product-minded' means every feature decision is connected to a user outcome AND a business metric — this is what separates a PM from a project manager or a business analyst

tasks.title: The Product Manager — Marty Cagan, SVPG
tasks.summary: Marty Cagan, founder of SVPG and author of Inspired, defines what a product manager is responsible for — and critically, what they are NOT responsible for — in a product-led company. This gives Pranav a second lens alongside Reforge: where Reforge is a skills and seniority map, SVPG is a responsibilities and accountability map.
keyTakeaways: The PM's core job is to discover a product that is simultaneously valuable (users want it), usable (users can use it), feasible (engineering can build it), and viable (the business should build it) — all four tests apply to every feature, not just new products

A PM does not own design, engineering, or data — but they are fully accountable for the outcome those teams produce together, which is a fundamentally different relationship than a manager or a consultant
The biggest failure mode for APMs is becoming a feature factory — executing a list of requests from stakeholders rather than discovering the right problem to solve first

tasks.keyword: Swiggy APM associate product manager day in life squad structure India 2024
tasks.whyItMatters: Reading frameworks gives you vocabulary; seeing how a real Indian APM spends their actual day gives you context for applying that vocabulary in practice. Look for blog posts, LinkedIn articles, or YouTube videos by Swiggy, Zomato, or Zepto PMs describing their daily workflow — focus on squad structure, weekly rituals (standups, design reviews, data reviews, stakeholder syncs), and how they handle competing priorities.
whatToLearn: What percentage of a PM's week is in meetings versus solo work — and what kinds of meetings they run (sprint planning, design review, data review, escalation calls, roadmap presentations)
How an Indian product team at a consumer internet company divides ownership between APM and PM — what the APM owns independently and what requires PM or leadership sign-off

tasks.promptTitle: Map 15 Swiggy PM tasks to the 4 Reforge pillars
tasks.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM coach helping me build a foundational understanding of the PM role using the Reforge 12-competency model.

Today I studied:
- Ravi Mehta's 12 PM competencies across 4 pillars: Product Execution, Customer Insight, Product Strategy, Influencing People
- Marty Cagan's definition of the PM's core job: discover a product that is valuable, usable, feasible, and viable
- How Swiggy organises its product team into squads with APMs embedded in specific feature areas

Real world context: Imagine I am an APM at Swiggy's consumer app squad. My squad owns the homepage feed, cart, and checkout experience.

Your role today: Test whether I understand what a PM actually does by having me categorise real PM tasks. Push back if I put something in the wrong pillar. Ask me WHY for every answer. Do not move on until I have justified my choice.

Activity — 15 PM tasks to categorise:
Below are 15 tasks from a typical Swiggy APM's week. I will tell you which of the 4 Reforge pillars each task belongs to. For each one, ask me to justify my answer before confirming. After all 15, give me a score and tell me where I was confused.

1. Write acceptance criteria for a new restaurant sorting algorithm
2. Review a Mixpanel funnel showing drop-off at the payment step
3. Interview 5 Swiggy users about why they abandon their cart
4. Write a product requirements document for a 'reorder last meal' button
5. Align with the engineering lead on sprint scope for next week
6. Present the Q3 product roadmap to the VP of Product
7. Define the North Star metric for the checkout squad
8. Review a designer's wireframe for the new address confirmation screen
9. Identify that app ratings dropped 0.3 stars after the last release
10. Make the case to leadership for prioritising checkout speed over new features
11. Run a team retrospective with engineering and design
12. Analyse a competitor app (Zomato) for differences in the checkout flow
13. Write a 1-pager on why Swiggy should build offline ordering for low-connectivity areas
14. Tell the data analyst exactly what query to run and why
15. Negotiate scope trade-offs with the engineering lead when the sprint is overloaded

After I respond to all 15, give feedback in EXACTLY this format:
WHAT WORKED: [bullets]
WHAT MISSED: [bullets]
INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE: [one insight about how Indian PM roles at consumer internet companies differ from US/global norms — especially the cross-functional dynamics at Swiggy, Zepto, or Razorpay]
COACHING NOTE: [one specific improvement tip for Pranav]

Begin with: 'Let's go task by task. Start with Task 1: Write acceptance criteria for a new restaurant sorting algorithm. Which of the 4 Reforge pillars does this belong to — and why?'

bonusTasks.title: Good Product Team / Failed Product Team — Marty Cagan, SVPG
bonusTasks.summary: Cagan's most-shared article draws a sharp contrast between empowered product teams (who own outcomes) and feature factories (who execute requests). Reading this after Day 1's core tasks reframes the PM role as a cultural and organisational choice — critical context for APMs joining large companies like Swiggy or Razorpay where both team types can exist in the same building.

bonusTasks.title: First-person APM experience at top Indian startups
bonusTasks.keyword: APM associate product manager first year experience Flipkart Razorpay Meesho blog 2023 2024
bonusTasks.summary: Search for first-person accounts of the APM role at top Indian companies. Look specifically for posts that describe the onboarding experience, what surprised them most, and what skills they wished they had built before joining. These personal stories add colour that no framework article can provide — and they often reveal informal skills (writing, meeting facilitation, data intuition) that matter as much as formal frameworks.

assessmentTask.taskDescription: Sort 20 PM tasks into the 4 Reforge pillars in a table with justifications, then write 3 sentences distinguishing what an APM at Swiggy does versus the engineering lead, the designer, and the data analyst on the same squad.
assessmentTask.rawMaterial:
Here are 20 tasks from a real Swiggy product team's sprint board and meeting calendar. Sort each into one of the 4 Reforge pillars (Product Execution / Customer Insight / Product Strategy / Influencing People):

1. Write user stories for a 'Schedule Order' feature
2. Analyse why Monday order volume is 18% below forecast
3. Run a 30-minute user interview with a Swiggy power user
4. Present product strategy for Q4 to the CPO
5. Create a wireframe feedback document for the design team
6. Prioritise 10 backlog features for the next sprint using RICE
7. Write the product brief for a new loyalty programme
8. Review NPS scores and cluster the most common complaints by theme
9. Write the launch checklist for a new payment method
10. Set success metrics for the new restaurant discovery feed
11. Negotiate scope trade-offs with the engineering lead
12. Analyse cohort retention data for users who used the new feed vs those who did not
13. Conduct a competitive audit of Zomato's restaurant page UX
14. Write a quarterly business review slide deck for the VP
15. Define edge cases for the address autocomplete feature
16. Facilitate a cross-team alignment meeting on API dependencies
17. Flag a quality regression in the checkout flow to the engineering team
18. Build the 6-month product roadmap for the consumer app
19. Write a 1-pager making the case for an offline ordering mode
20. Review and sign off on the sprint demo before the release goes live
assessmentTask.outputFormat: Part 1: A table with 4 columns — Task Number | Task Description | Reforge Pillar | Justification (1 sentence). Part 2: Three separate sentences, each starting with 'Unlike the [role], the APM...' — one sentence each for engineering lead, designer, and data analyst.
assessmentTask.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM reviewing my work on Day 1: What the PM role actually is.

I studied today:
- Ravi Mehta's Reforge 12 PM Competency Model — 4 pillars, 12 competencies, 3 seniority levels
- Marty Cagan's SVPG definition of the PM's core job: valuable, usable, feasible, viable
- How Swiggy's product team is structured into squads with APMs embedded in specific feature areas

Real world context I should apply:
Swiggy APMs are embedded in squads (consumer app, logistics, restaurant-partner tools, growth) and own specific feature areas. They work daily with one engineering lead, one designer, and one analyst.

MY TASK TODAY WAS: Sort 20 Swiggy PM tasks into the 4 Reforge pillars and write 3 sentences distinguishing the APM role from engineering lead, designer, and data analyst.

Here is my work:
[Pranav pastes his table and 3-sentence role distinction here]

Review my work and give feedback in EXACTLY this format — do not change these headers:

WHAT I GOT RIGHT:
- [specific bullets with explanation]

WHAT NEEDS CORRECTION:
- [specific bullets with explanation of why]

BLIND SPOTS:
- [things I did not consider at all]

INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE:
- [one insight about Indian product team structures at consumer internet companies like Swiggy — e.g., how the PM-engineering relationship differs from Silicon Valley norms, or the role of ops and growth teams that are unusually influential in Indian companies]

OPEN POINTS FOR TOMORROW:
- [1-3 specific, actionable items only]

SCORE: [X/10]
REFORGE COMPETENCY: Feature Specification — [Needs Focus / On Track / Outperform]

days.theme: Structured thinking — MECE and first principles
days.objective: Build a correctly structured MECE tree for a real product problem, place 6 provided causes into the correct branches without overlap, identify the first branch to investigate with justification, and challenge one provided assumption using first-principles reasoning.

realWorldAnchor.example: When Zepto evaluated expanding from metro dark stores into Tier 2 cities, the decision required first-principles reasoning — not 'Blinkit is doing it so we should too' but a ground-up decomposition of whether unit economics, demand density, and supply chain assumptions that held in Mumbai would hold in Nagpur.

days.teamContext: Structured thinking is most visible in the PM's interactions with Engineering leads during problem scoping — when a PM can decompose a problem into mutually exclusive branches, engineering scope becomes cleaner, trade-offs become explicit, and design priorities become obvious to the whole team.

tasks.title: MECE: The Framework That Makes You Think Like a Consultant — Product School
tasks.summary: This article explains the MECE principle (Mutually Exclusive, Collectively Exhaustive) as a thinking tool specifically for product managers — how to decompose problems into branches that have no overlap and no gaps. For Pranav, who has consulting exposure, MECE will feel structurally familiar, but this article adds the product-specific application: breaking down a metric drop, a user complaint spike, or a prioritisation trade-off.
keyTakeaways: MECE means every branch of your breakdown is non-overlapping (mutually exclusive) AND together the branches cover every possibility without exception (collectively exhaustive) — violating either rule produces a flawed analysis that will mislead the team

In PM work, MECE trees are most useful in three situations: diagnosing a metric drop (what could have caused this?), segmenting a user base for feature prioritisation, and structuring a product strategy recommendation to leadership

The hardest discipline in MECE is avoiding the intuitive jump to solutions — your tree branches must describe the problem space completely before you touch solutions, otherwise you will solve the wrong branch

tasks.title: First Principles Thinking — Farnam Street
tasks.summary: Farnam Street's definitive explainer on first principles reasoning — the method of breaking a problem down to its most fundamental truths and building back up from there, rather than reasoning by analogy from what others have done. This is the thinking method behind Jio's free data launch, Zepto's 10-minute delivery model, and CRED's credit-score-only user filter — each was a first-principles bet made by questioning an assumption everyone else accepted.
keyTakeaways: Reasoning by analogy ('Swiggy did X so we should do X') is fast but caps innovation — first principles forces the question: is this assumption actually true, or have we inherited it from someone who inherited it from someone else?

A practical first-principles test for PMs has three questions: (1) Is this actually true? (2) Is it always true regardless of context? (3) Does it have to be true, or is it only true given current constraints that might change?

Jio's 2016 decision to give away free data at scale is the most cited Indian business example of first principles — Mukesh Ambani questioned the assumption that data must be priced as a premium good, concluded it was false given Jio's cost structure, and restructured Indian telecom permanently

tasks.keyword: MECE tree metric drop product manager worked example diagnosis walkthrough PM interview
tasks.whyItMatters: Reading about MECE is the first step; seeing a PM use MECE to walk through a specific metric drop — with the full tree drawn out, branches named, and a hypothesis selected — is where the concept becomes usable. Look for blog walkthroughs or YouTube videos that show the tree visually, not just explain it abstractly.
whatToLearn: What the top-level branches of a metric drop MECE tree typically look like — the most common structures are: internal vs external causes; supply-side vs demand-side; technical vs behavioural; or a funnel-stage breakdown (acquisition / activation / retention / revenue)

How a PM selects which branch to investigate first — the answer combines three factors: which is most likely given available context, which is fastest to verify with existing data, and which has the biggest business impact if the hypothesis is confirmed

tasks.promptTitle: Build a MECE tree for Zomato's Monday order drop
tasks.claudePrompt:
You are a PM coach helping me practice MECE structured thinking for product problem diagnosis.

Today I studied:
- The MECE principle: Mutually Exclusive, Collectively Exhaustive — used to decompose problems without gaps or overlaps
- First principles reasoning: breaking assumptions down to fundamental truths rather than reasoning by analogy
- How Zepto used first principles to evaluate Tier 2 expansion, and how Jio used it to restructure Indian telecom pricing assumptions

Real world context: Zomato's Monday morning order volume dropped 22% compared to last Monday. The CEO wants an explanation by end of day. No additional data has been provided yet.

Your role today: Act as my skeptical PM mentor. When I build my MECE tree, challenge me on every branch — is this truly mutually exclusive from the other branches? Does the tree cover everything plausible? Have I missed an entire category? Push me to think one level deeper, not just validate my first answer.

Activity — 3 rounds:
Round 1: I state my top-level branches (2-4 categories) and explain why they are MECE.
Round 2: For the branch I identify as most likely, I expand it into sub-branches.
Round 3: I pick the most investigable sub-branch and name 3 specific data checks I would run.

After all 3 rounds, give feedback in EXACTLY this format:
WHAT WORKED: [bullets]
WHAT MISSED: [bullets]
INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE: [one insight — Monday order patterns in India are shaped by specific local factors: weekly prepaid recharge cycles, salary credit dates, weather patterns affecting delivery areas, and Monday-after-a-holiday effects — a non-India PM might not know to ask about these]
COACHING NOTE: [one specific improvement tip for Pranav]

Begin with: 'Before you build the tree — tell me in one sentence: what question is your MECE tree trying to answer? Then give me your top-level branches.'

bonusTasks.title: Jio first-principles deep dive
bonusTasks.keyword: Jio 2016 free data launch Mukesh Ambani first principles reasoning telecom disruption India product strategy
bonusTasks.summary: Jio's entry into Indian telecom is one of the most documented first-principles product decisions in Indian business history — a company that questioned the fundamental pricing assumption of an entire industry and was proven right at a scale that reshaped half a billion people's internet access. This search gives Pranav a deeply documented real example of what first principles looks like when applied at the highest level. Look for analysis on inc42.com, YourStory, The Ken, or long-form economic newspaper pieces.

assessmentTask.taskDescription: Build a complete 2-level MECE tree for Zomato's 22% Monday order drop, place all 6 provided causes into the correct branches, identify the first branch to investigate with justification, and use first-principles reasoning to challenge one of the 6 provided assumptions.
assessmentTask.rawMaterial:
Zomato's Monday order volume dropped 22% compared to last Monday. Here are 6 possible causes flagged by different team members:

1. The payment gateway had 400ms higher latency on Monday morning (flagged by engineering)
2. It rained heavily in Delhi and Mumbai on Sunday night (flagged by operations)
3. Zomato ran a 40% discount campaign last Monday but not this Monday (flagged by marketing)
4. Three popular restaurant chains in Mumbai went offline simultaneously (flagged by restaurant partnerships)
5. A competitor (Swiggy) launched a 'Free Delivery Monday' promo this week (flagged by growth team)
6. The iOS app had a crash on the restaurant search screen affecting users on app version 14.2 (flagged by engineering)

Your deliverables:
A. Build a MECE tree with at least 2 levels. Top-level branches must cover all 6 causes above without any overlap. Place each of the 6 causes under the correct branch — no cause should appear in two branches.
B. Identify which top-level branch you would investigate first. Justify in 3 sentences using: likelihood given available context, speed of verification, and business impact if true.
C. Using first-principles reasoning, identify ONE assumption in the list above that you would question before accepting as fact. State what you would verify and why the assumption might be wrong.
assessmentTask.outputFormat: Part A: A visual tree using indentation or dashes — top-level branches clearly labelled, sub-branches indented, each of the 6 causes placed at the leaf level with its number. Part B: Three sentences of justification (one each for: likelihood, verification speed, business impact). Part C: One assumption named + a 2-sentence explanation of why you would verify it before accepting it.
assessmentTask.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM reviewing my work on Day 2: Structured Thinking — MECE and First Principles.

I studied today:
- MECE principle: Mutually Exclusive, Collectively Exhaustive — used to decompose product problems without gaps or overlaps
- First principles reasoning: questioning assumptions rather than accepting them — the Jio and Zepto examples
- How to select which MECE branch to investigate first: likelihood, verification speed, business impact

Real world context I should apply:
Zomato's 22% Monday order drop — a realistic PM diagnosis scenario with 6 flagged causes from different teams.

MY TASK TODAY WAS: Build a 2-level MECE tree, place 6 causes correctly, justify which branch to investigate first, and challenge one assumption using first principles.

Here is my work:
[Pranav pastes his MECE tree, branch justification, and first-principles challenge here]

Review my work and give feedback in EXACTLY this format — do not change these headers:

WHAT I GOT RIGHT:
- [specific bullets with explanation]

WHAT NEEDS CORRECTION:
- [specific bullets with explanation of why — especially check for MECE violations: any cause appearing in two branches, or any top-level category that is missing]

BLIND SPOTS:
- [things I did not consider at all]

INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE:
- [one insight about how Monday order patterns in India are shaped by specific local factors — weekly prepaid recharge cycles, salary credit dates, post-Sunday weather, regional holidays — that a non-India PM would miss]

OPEN POINTS FOR TOMORROW:
- [1-3 specific, actionable items only]

SCORE: [X/10]
REFORGE COMPETENCY: Feature Specification — [Needs Focus / On Track / Outperform]

days.objective: Write a specific product persona from a real app store complaint, identify the underlying Job To Be Done using the correct JTBD format, and propose one feature with a measurable success metric.

realWorldAnchor.example: CRED's decision to restrict membership exclusively to users with credit scores above 750 was not a technical constraint — it was a deliberate user segmentation choice based on a specific JTBD: 'help me feel recognised and rewarded for the financial discipline I already practise,' which Kunal Shah believed only high-credit-score users would authentically experience.

days.teamContext: Personas and JTBD are most directly used in the PM's collaboration with the Design team — PMs use these frameworks to align designers on exactly who they are building for, preventing the most common design failure: building for an abstract average user rather than a specific person in a specific situation.

tasks.title: Jobs-to-be-Done: A Framework for Customer Needs — Product School
tasks.summary: This article explains Clayton Christensen's Jobs-To-Be-Done framework for product managers — the idea that users do not buy products or use features; they hire them to accomplish a specific job in their life. For Pranav, JTBD reframes the question from 'what features should we build?' to 'what job is the user trying to get done, and are we the best tool for that job?' — a shift that changes every prioritisation and design decision downstream.
keyTakeaways: The classic JTBD example: people do not buy a drill — they hire it to make a hole, because they need a shelf mounted before guests arrive. The real job is the shelf, not the hole. A PM who understands the real job might build a modular shelf system instead of a better drill

JTBD replaces demographic personas with situational ones: not 'a 28-year-old urban professional' but 'someone who just moved into a new flat and needs it to feel like home before the weekend' — the situation, not the age bracket, drives the job

For Indian fintech (CRED, PhonePe, Razorpay), JTBD forces the question: are users hiring the payment app to transact efficiently, to feel in control of their money, or to be rewarded for behaviour they already practise? Each is a different job and leads to a completely different product

tasks.title: How to Create User Personas That Drive Real Product Decisions — Product School
tasks.summary: This article explains how to build a useful product persona — not a marketing persona full of demographics, but a product persona focused on the user's goals, pain points, mental models, and behaviours relevant to the product. The article distinguishes between proto-personas (hypothesis-based) and research-backed personas, and explains when each is appropriate for different stages of product development.
keyTakeaways: A useful product persona has four non-negotiable components: who they are in context (not demographics), what they want to accomplish (goal), what blocks them (pain), and how they currently cope (existing workaround) — missing any one makes the persona useless for design decisions

Demographic personas ('Priya, 27, Mumbai, IT sector') are nearly useless for product decisions; situational personas ('Priya is trying to split a dinner bill quickly without awkwardness while standing at the restaurant counter') drive real design choices because they specify the exact moment of friction

At companies like Zepto or CRED, a PM typically maintains 2-3 primary personas — more than three and the team loses focus; fewer than two and you miss critical use case differences that will cause one segment to churn

tasks.keyword: CRED Kunal Shah user segmentation 750 credit score product strategy reasoning interview 2022 2023
tasks.whyItMatters: CRED's decision to restrict membership to users with 750+ credit scores is a real, publicly debated product and strategy call that Kunal Shah has explained in interviews and talks — searching for his reasoning gives Pranav a master class in deliberate user segmentation, the antithesis of 'build for everyone.' Look for podcast interviews, LinkedIn posts, or long-form articles where Kunal Shah explains the CRED user thesis in his own words.
whatToLearn: Why restricting your user base can be a strategic product advantage rather than a growth ceiling — and the specific conditions under which this logic holds versus when it becomes a self-imposed constraint that limits scale

How Kunal Shah used JTBD-style thinking ('what job does a financially disciplined user want done that no product is currently serving well?') to define CRED's initial product direction and resist pressure to expand to lower credit score users

tasks.promptTitle: Build persona, JTBD, and feature proposal from a Zepto complaint
tasks.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM helping me practice translating real user complaints into actionable product personas and Jobs-To-Be-Done statements.

Today I studied:
- JTBD framework: users hire products to accomplish a situational job — the job is defined by the situation, not the demographic
- Product persona structure: context (who they are in situation), goal (what they want), pain (what blocks them), existing workaround (how they cope today)
- CRED's deliberate user segmentation: only users with 750+ credit scores, because the JTBD was 'feel recognised for financial discipline already practised'

Real world context: I am a PM on Zepto's consumer app squad. I have just received a batch of App Store reviews. My job is to extract a persona, a JTBD, and a feature proposal from a specific review.

Your role today: Act as a PM mentor who pushes me to go deeper. When I write a persona, ask: 'What would you actually build for this person — and what would you NOT build?' When I write a JTBD, ask: 'Is that the real job, or is it a symptom of a deeper job?' Do not accept surface-level answers.

Activity:

Here are 5 Zepto App Store reviews:

Review 1 (1 star): 'Charged me twice for one order. Customer care took 3 days to refund. Never using again.'
Review 2 (2 stars): 'Good app but delivery is often late. 10 minutes means 25 minutes in my area.'
Review 3 (3 stars): 'I use Zepto every day for my morning groceries but I have to retype the same items every single time. There is no saved list or easy reorder. So annoying for someone who buys the same things.'
Review 4 (4 stars): 'Fast delivery, great selection. Wish there were more organic options.'
Review 5 (2 stars): 'The app keeps crashing on my phone (Redmi Note 11). Very frustrating.'

We will work on Review 3 only.

Step 1: I write a persona for the user behind Review 3. You push back if I use demographics without situational context, or if any of the four components is missing or vague.
Step 2: I write a JTBD statement in this exact format: 'When [situation], I want to [motivation/goal], so I can [expected outcome].' You push back if the job is surface-level.
Step 3: I propose one specific Zepto feature that directly addresses the JTBD. I include a success metric in the format: 'We will know this worked if [measurable outcome] within [time period].'

After all 3 steps, give feedback in EXACTLY this format:
WHAT WORKED: [bullets]
WHAT MISSED: [bullets]
INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE: [one insight — daily grocery buying in Indian households follows a very different pattern than Western weekly grocery shopping: the ration list, the kirana store mental model, the household structure where the same 15-20 staples are bought on a near-daily basis — Zepto's repeat-purchase problem has specific Indian household context]
COACHING NOTE: [one specific improvement tip for Pranav]

Begin with: 'Good. Start with Step 1. Write me a persona for the user behind Review 3. Remember: context in their situation, goal, pain, existing workaround. No demographics unless they are directly relevant to the product decision. Go.'

bonusTasks.title: Why Dunzo failed — JTBD analysis
bonusTasks.keyword: Dunzo failure reasons product strategy user value proposition India 2023 2024 post-mortem on-demand delivery
bonusTasks.summary: Dunzo was once considered a pioneer of on-demand delivery in India but failed to achieve the scale of Swiggy, Zepto, or Blinkit despite an early head start. Analysing its failure through the JTBD lens — did users have a clearly defined job they were hiring Dunzo for? Was that job valuable enough to pay for without subsidies? — gives Pranav a real counter-example to CRED's sharp user segmentation. Look for post-mortems on inc42.com, Mint, The Ken, or Finshots.

assessmentTask.taskDescription: Write a complete product persona from Zepto Review 3, write a JTBD statement in the specified format, and propose one feature with a name, 2-sentence description, and a measurable success metric.
assessmentTask.rawMaterial:
Here are 5 Zepto App Store reviews. Work with Review 3 only:

Review 1 (1 star): 'Charged me twice for one order. Customer care took 3 days to refund. Never using again.'
Review 2 (2 stars): 'Good app but delivery is often late. 10 minutes means 25 minutes in my area.'
Review 3 (3 stars): 'I use Zepto every day for my morning groceries but I have to retype the same items every single time. There is no saved list or easy reorder. So annoying for someone who buys the same things.'
Review 4 (4 stars): 'Fast delivery, great selection. Wish there were more organic options.'
Review 5 (2 stars): 'The app keeps crashing on my phone (Redmi Note 11). Very frustrating.'

For Review 3 only, produce:
1. PERSONA — Include all four components: Context (who is this person and what is their daily pattern?), Goal (what are they trying to accomplish in the moment?), Pain (what specific friction do they experience?), Existing Workaround (how do they cope with this friction today before a fix exists?).
2. JTBD STATEMENT — Use exactly this format: 'When [situation], I want to [motivation/goal], so I can [expected outcome].'
3. FEATURE PROPOSAL — Name it. Describe it in 2 sentences. Write a success metric in this format: 'We will know this worked if [measurable outcome] within [time period].'
assessmentTask.outputFormat: Three clearly labelled sections: PERSONA (4 bullet points: Context / Goal / Pain / Existing Workaround) | JTBD (one formatted statement) | FEATURE PROPOSAL (name + 2-sentence description + success metric).
assessmentTask.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM reviewing my work on Day 3: User Thinking — Personas and Jobs-To-Be-Done.

I studied today:
- Jobs-To-Be-Done framework: users hire products for situational jobs — the format is: 'When [situation], I want to [goal], so I can [outcome]'
- Product persona structure: context, goal, pain, existing workaround — all four required
- CRED's user segmentation strategy: deliberately targeting users with 750+ credit scores because of a specific JTBD only they would experience authentically

Real world context I should apply:
Zepto's repeat-purchase problem — a daily user frustrated by retyping the same grocery list every single day, reflecting a common Indian household pattern where the same 15-20 staples are bought nearly every day rather than in a weekly shop.

MY TASK TODAY WAS: Write a product persona from Zepto Review 3, write a JTBD statement, and propose one feature with a success metric.

Here is my work:
[Pranav pastes his Persona, JTBD statement, and Feature Proposal here]

Review my work and give feedback in EXACTLY this format — do not change these headers:

WHAT I GOT RIGHT:
- [specific bullets with explanation]

WHAT NEEDS CORRECTION:
- [specific bullets with explanation of why]

BLIND SPOTS:
- [things I did not consider at all — e.g., the difference between a 'saved list' and a 'smart reorder' feature; whether the job is about speed or cognitive load; what the existing workaround actually is for most Indian daily grocery buyers]

INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE:
- [one insight about daily grocery buying patterns in Indian households — the ration list, the kirana store mental model, and how Zepto's repeat-purchase friction is specifically an Indian household problem that would not appear in Western weekly grocery shopping contexts]

OPEN POINTS FOR TOMORROW:
- [1-3 specific, actionable items only]

SCORE: [X/10]
REFORGE COMPETENCY: Voice of Customer — [Needs Focus / On Track / Outperform]

days.objective: Reframe three vague problem statements into properly scoped statements using all four required components, and write a 5-question user interview script for one of them using only open-ended, behaviour-based questions.

realWorldAnchor.example: Razorpay's product team reportedly reframed payment failures from 'a technical reliability problem' to 'a trust breakdown at the exact moment a user most needs the product to work' — this reframe shifted the solution space from purely infrastructure fixes to UX interventions: real-time failure explanations, instant retry flows, and visible settlement timelines that rebuilt user confidence.

days.teamContext: Problem framing is where the PM's relationship with the Data team becomes most critical — a well-scoped problem statement tells the analyst exactly what data to pull and what anomaly to look for, while a vague problem statement results in weeks of back-and-forth pulling the wrong metrics.

tasks.title: How to Write a Product Problem Statement — Product School
tasks.summary: This article walks through how to write a product problem statement that is specific enough to be actionable — covering the four required components (user in situation, goal, pain, business impact) and showing examples of vague versus well-scoped statements side by side. For Pranav, this directly bridges yesterday's JTBD work with today's skill: a JTBD describes the job the user wants done; a problem statement describes the gap between the current product and that job being done well.
keyTakeaways: A problem statement requires four components: who the user is in a specific situation (not a demographic), what they are trying to accomplish (specific goal), what is blocking them (specific friction or pain), and why it matters to the business (a metric or outcome that is affected) — missing any one makes it unactionable for engineering and design

Vague problem statements generate vague solutions: 'users are not engaging' has infinite possible solutions; 'first-time UPI users in Tier 2 cities abandon at the payment confirmation screen because they distrust whether the payment went through' generates a specific, testable intervention

The business impact component is the most commonly skipped — but without it, the PM cannot prioritise this problem against competing problems, and engineers and designers have no sense of urgency or strategic relevance

tasks.title: How to Run Effective User Interviews — Lenny's Newsletter
tasks.summary: Lenny Rachitsky covers how to design and run user interviews that produce genuine behavioural insight rather than confirmation of existing hypotheses. The article covers question design, interview structure, and how to synthesize findings into actionable product insights — a practitioner-level guide that APMs can apply to user calls starting this week.
keyTakeaways: The most common interview mistake is asking 'would you use this feature?' — users almost always say yes to hypotheticals because they want to be helpful. Instead ask: 'Tell me about the last time you tried to do X. What actually happened?' — behaviour-based questions reveal real patterns, not ideals
Five well-run user interviews produce more actionable insight than 50 survey responses — qualitative interviews reveal the 'why' behind the 'what' that surveys can only count

In Indian markets, user interview design requires two additional decisions: language choice (English vs vernacular) and setting (in-person vs phone), because self-reported behaviour in formal English interviews often differs substantially from actual usage behaviour in the product's natural context

tasks.keyword: Razorpay payment failure trust UX product decisions India fintech blog 2021 2022
tasks.whyItMatters: Razorpay's reframe of payment failure as a trust problem — not just a technical reliability problem — is a documented product philosophy shift that influenced their UX, content design, and communication decisions. Searching for articles or talks by their product team shows what problem reframing looks like in practice at a real Indian fintech.
whatToLearn: What specific UX interventions Razorpay introduced because of the trust-reframe — real-time status indicators, settlement visibility timelines, failure explanation messages — and how each maps back to the reframed problem statement rather than the original technical framing

How the trust-reframe changed which teams owned the solution: it moved responsibility from purely Engineering to a cross-functional problem requiring PM, Design, and Content Design — which is itself a lesson in how problem framing determines team structure

tasks.promptTitle: Reframe 3 vague problem statements + write a user interview script
tasks.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM helping me practice problem framing — the skill of turning vague observations into specific, actionable product problem statements.

Today I studied:
- The 4-component problem statement: user in specific situation / specific goal / specific pain / measurable business impact
- How to design user interviews that reveal behaviour rather than confirm hypotheses — open-ended, behaviour-based questions, not hypotheticals
- How Razorpay reframed payment failures as a trust problem, not a technical problem — and what that reframe unlocked in terms of solution space

Real world context: I am an APM at a fintech company similar to Razorpay. Three problem statements have been written by different team members in a planning meeting. All three are vague and need reframing.

Your role today: Review my reframed problem statements as a strict senior PM. For each one, if I miss a component (user in situation / goal / pain / business impact), tell me exactly which one is missing and why it matters. After problem reframing, help me write a 5-question interview script for Problem 2.

The three vague problem statements:

PROBLEM 1: 'Users are not completing transactions on our app.'
PROBLEM 2: 'We need to improve our onboarding.'
PROBLEM 3: 'The product has too many steps and users get confused.'

For each problem statement:
Step A: I reframe it with all 4 components using this format: 'When [user in specific situation] tries to [specific goal], they experience [specific pain], which results in [measurable business impact].'
Step B: You review and push back on anything missing, vague, or imprecise.

After all 3 reframes are approved, I write a 5-question user interview script specifically for Problem 2 (the onboarding problem). You review the script and flag any questions that are: leading, closed (yes/no only), or hypothetical rather than behavioural.

After all work is reviewed, give feedback in EXACTLY this format:
WHAT WORKED: [bullets]
WHAT MISSED: [bullets]
INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE: [one insight — onboarding problems in Indian fintech often have a specific extra layer that Western problem statements would miss: KYC friction, Aadhaar OTP failure rates, first-time digital user anxiety, or the difference between English and vernacular instruction comprehension]
COACHING NOTE: [one specific improvement tip for Pranav]

Begin with: 'Let's start with Problem 1. Read it carefully: "Users are not completing transactions on our app." Now reframe it with all 4 components. Write it as a single structured sentence using the format I gave you. Go.'

bonusTasks.title: PhonePe Tier 2 user research methods
bonusTasks.keyword: PhonePe Tier 2 Tier 3 user research Hindi UPI adoption first time digital user product insights India
bonusTasks.summary: PhonePe's expansion into Tier 2 and Tier 3 cities required a fundamentally different approach to user research — factory workers, small traders, and first-time digital users who communicated differently and whose actual usage patterns contradicted what they said in formal sessions. This search directly extends today's user interview reading into Indian market-specific research practice. Look for PhonePe blog posts, PM talks, or inc42/YourStory coverage of their regional expansion product strategy.

assessmentTask.taskDescription: Reframe all 3 vague problem statements into properly scoped statements with all 4 components, then write a 5-question user interview script for Problem 2 — every question must be open-ended and behaviour-based.
assessmentTask.rawMaterial:
You are an APM at a company similar to Razorpay. These 3 problem statements were written by different team members in a product planning meeting:

PROBLEM 1: 'Users are not completing transactions on our app.'
PROBLEM 2: 'We need to improve our onboarding.'
PROBLEM 3: 'The product has too many steps and users get confused.'

For each problem statement, reframe it using this exact structure:
'When [user in specific situation] tries to [specific goal], they experience [specific pain], which results in [measurable business impact].'

For Problem 2 only:
Also write a 5-question user interview script. Rules for every question: open-ended (cannot be answered with yes/no), behaviour-based (asks about what the user actually did, not what they would do), not leading (does not suggest the answer). Include a 1-sentence note after each question: 'What this reveals: [the specific insight this question is designed to surface].'
assessmentTask.outputFormat: Part 1: Three labelled problem statements, each in the specified 4-component format. Part 2: A numbered list of 5 interview questions, each followed by a 1-sentence 'What this reveals:' note.
assessmentTask.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM reviewing my work on Day 4: Problem Framing and User Research.

I studied today:
- The 4-component problem statement structure: user in specific situation / specific goal / specific pain / measurable business impact
- User interview design: open-ended, behaviour-based questions — never hypothetical or leading
- Razorpay's trust reframe: how reframing a payment failure as a trust problem (not an infrastructure problem) unlocked UX and content design solutions that pure engineering fixes could not provide

Real world context I should apply:
I am an APM at a fintech similar to Razorpay. Three vague problem statements need reframing. For Problem 2 (onboarding), I also wrote a 5-question user interview script.

MY TASK TODAY WAS: Reframe 3 problem statements with all 4 components and write a 5-question behaviour-based interview script for Problem 2.

Here is my work:
[Pranav pastes his 3 reframed problem statements and interview script here]

Review my work and give feedback in EXACTLY this format — do not change these headers:

WHAT I GOT RIGHT:
- [specific bullets with explanation]

WHAT NEEDS CORRECTION:
- [specific bullets with explanation of why]

BLIND SPOTS:
