days.theme: Prioritisation frameworks — RICE, ICE, and MoSCoW
days.objective: Score 8 PhonePe features using the RICE formula with full arithmetic working shown for each, rank all 8, select the top 3 for a pre-Diwali launch with justification, and give one-sentence business reasoning for dropping each of the remaining 5.

realWorldAnchor.example: Before a Diwali or IPL season launch, a Swiggy PM runs through the backlog using a prioritisation framework to decide which features get engineering time — festive seasons represent disproportionate revenue and user acquisition in India, meaning the Reach and Confidence inputs in RICE shift significantly for features that serve high-volume moments.

days.teamContext: Prioritisation decisions directly involve Engineering (who estimates Effort in person-months) and Marketing/Growth (who provides Reach estimates and impact context for campaign-linked features) — the PM's job is to synthesize inputs from both teams into a scored framework, not to decide unilaterally.

tasks.title: RICE: Simple Prioritization for Product Managers — Intercom
tasks.summary: This is the original blog post from Intercom that introduced and defined the RICE scoring model — Reach, Impact, Confidence, and Effort. Intercom's PM team created RICE to solve a specific internal problem: too many competing priorities and no shared scoring language across teams. The article explains the formula precisely, defines each variable with examples, and shows worked calculations. This is the definitive primary source for the framework.
keyTakeaways: RICE Score = (Reach × Impact × Confidence%) divided by Effort in person-months — the formula must be applied consistently across all features being compared, otherwise the resulting scores cannot be ranked against each other

Confidence is the most underused and most important variable: it forces honesty about how well the team actually knows the Reach and Impact estimates, preventing overconfident bets on features that are exciting but untested

RICE works best for comparing features within the same product area and timeframe — it breaks down when comparing a speculative 0-to-1 feature (low confidence, high potential) against an incremental improvement (high confidence, modest impact), because those are fundamentally different types of bets

tasks.title: How to Prioritize Product Features — Lenny's Newsletter
tasks.summary: Lenny Rachitsky surveys how top product teams at companies like Airbnb, Figma, and Duolingo actually prioritise features — not just what framework they use, but how they balance quantitative scoring with strategic judgment and when they override the numbers. The article is especially useful for understanding the limits of RICE and the most common prioritisation mistakes.
keyTakeaways: No framework replaces strategic judgment: a feature with a high RICE score that contradicts the company's current strategic focus should still be deprioritised — frameworks inform decisions, they do not make them

The three most common RICE mistakes: treating effort estimates from engineers as precise (they are wide estimates), forgetting that Reach estimates decay as the product matures, and conflating impact on users with impact on the specific business metric you are trying to move this quarter

MoSCoW (Must Have / Should Have / Could Have / Won't Have) is most useful for scoping a specific release, not for cross-feature portfolio prioritisation — use RICE to decide what to build across the quarter, use MoSCoW to decide what ships in the next sprint

tasks.keyword: RICE prioritization worked example product manager feature scoring table India startup walkthrough
tasks.whyItMatters: Seeing a PM walk through RICE on a realistic feature list — with actual numbers in a table, arithmetic shown, and final rankings justified — is where the framework becomes something Pranav can use rather than just describe. Look for blog posts or PM prep resources that show a full scoring table with working, not just the formula definition.
whatToLearn: How experienced PMs estimate Reach when exact data is not available — the acceptable approximation methods (monthly active users × estimated feature touch rate), and how to be explicit about assumptions so they can be challenged

What a final RICE output looks like structurally: a table with all features ranked by score, followed by a short narrative justifying the top choices and explaining why high-scoring features were still dropped for strategic reasons

tasks.promptTitle: RICE score 8 PhonePe features — live coaching with a skeptical PM lead
tasks.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM lead coaching me through a RICE scoring exercise for 8 PhonePe features.

Today I studied:
- RICE framework: Reach × Impact × Confidence% divided by Effort in person-months
- When to override RICE with strategic judgment and why
- MoSCoW as a release scoping tool (distinct from RICE as a portfolio prioritisation tool)

Real world context: I am an APM at PhonePe. It is September, eight weeks before Diwali. Engineering has capacity for approximately 3 features before the festive season. I need to present a prioritised shortlist to the PM lead today.

Your role today: Play the skeptical PM lead. When I score each feature, question my Reach estimate ('where did that number come from?'), my Confidence ('are you really 80% confident — what evidence do you have?'), and my Effort estimate ('have you actually talked to engineering, or is this your guess?'). Do not let me get away with undefended numbers.

The 8 features with input data:

Feature 1: UPI payment via WhatsApp integration
Reach: 5,000,000 users/month | Impact: 3 (high) | Confidence: 70% | Effort: 8 person-months

Feature 2: Credit score display on home dashboard
Reach: 8,000,000 users/month | Impact: 2 (medium) | Confidence: 90% | Effort: 2 person-months

Feature 3: Split bill feature for friend groups
Reach: 2,000,000 users/month | Impact: 2 (medium) | Confidence: 60% | Effort: 6 person-months

Feature 4: Voice commands in Hindi for payments
Reach: 12,000,000 users/month | Impact: 3 (high) | Confidence: 50% | Effort: 12 person-months

Feature 5: Auto-pay setup wizard for utility bills
Reach: 6,000,000 users/month | Impact: 3 (high) | Confidence: 80% | Effort: 4 person-months

Feature 6: Cashback gamification for regular users
Reach: 10,000,000 users/month | Impact: 2 (medium) | Confidence: 75% | Effort: 5 person-months

Feature 7: Offline NFC tap-to-pay
Reach: 3,000,000 users/month | Impact: 3 (high) | Confidence: 40% | Effort: 14 person-months

Feature 8: Cross-border remittance to Nepal and Bangladesh
Reach: 1,000,000 users/month | Impact: 3 (high) | Confidence: 55% | Effort: 10 person-months

Activity:
Step 1: I calculate the RICE score for each feature and show my working (full formula with numbers).
Step 2: I rank all 8 by RICE score.
Step 3: I pick the top 3 for pre-Diwali launch and justify, then explain why I am dropping the others.
You challenge my numbers at each step before I move on.

After all steps, give feedback in EXACTLY this format:
WHAT WORKED: [bullets]
WHAT MISSED: [bullets]
INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE: [one insight — Hindi voice commands and offline NFC have very different strategic value in India's festive season context and in Tier 2/3 market expansion than their RICE scores alone suggest — the strategic context changes the real priority even if the numbers do not]
COACHING NOTE: [one specific improvement tip for Pranav]

Begin with: 'Start with Feature 1: UPI via WhatsApp. Show me the formula and the number. Then tell me — where exactly did the Reach estimate of 5 million come from?'

bonusTasks.title: MoSCoW prioritisation framework explained — Product School
bonusTasks.summary: This Product School article explains MoSCoW (Must Have, Should Have, Could Have, Won't Have) using a realistic launch scenario. Reading this after RICE sharpens the mental model of which framework to use when: RICE for cross-feature portfolio decisions across a quarter, MoSCoW for scoping what ships in a specific release. Choosing the right framework for the right context is itself a signal of PM maturity in interviews.

assessmentTask.taskDescription: Score all 8 PhonePe features using the RICE formula with full arithmetic working shown for each, rank them 1-8, select the top 3 for pre-Diwali launch with 2-sentence justification each, and drop the bottom 5 with 1-sentence business reasoning each.
assessmentTask.rawMaterial:
You are an APM at PhonePe. It is September — 8 weeks before Diwali. Engineering has capacity for approximately 3 features. Score these 8 features using RICE (formula: Reach × Impact × Confidence% divided by Effort in person-months):

Feature 1: UPI via WhatsApp integration — Reach: 5,000,000 | Impact: 3 | Confidence: 70% | Effort: 8 person-months
Feature 2: Credit score on home dashboard — Reach: 8,000,000 | Impact: 2 | Confidence: 90% | Effort: 2 person-months
Feature 3: Split bill for friend groups — Reach: 2,000,000 | Impact: 2 | Confidence: 60% | Effort: 6 person-months
Feature 4: Hindi voice commands for payments — Reach: 12,000,000 | Impact: 3 | Confidence: 50% | Effort: 12 person-months
Feature 5: Auto-pay utility bills wizard — Reach: 6,000,000 | Impact: 3 | Confidence: 80% | Effort: 4 person-months
Feature 6: Cashback gamification for regulars — Reach: 10,000,000 | Impact: 2 | Confidence: 75% | Effort: 5 person-months
Feature 7: Offline NFC tap-to-pay — Reach: 3,000,000 | Impact: 3 | Confidence: 40% | Effort: 14 person-months
Feature 8: Cross-border remittance Nepal/Bangladesh — Reach: 1,000,000 | Impact: 3 | Confidence: 55% | Effort: 10 person-months
assessmentTask.outputFormat: Part 1: A table with columns: Feature | Reach | Impact | Confidence | Effort | RICE Score | Working (show the arithmetic). Part 2: All 8 features ranked highest to lowest RICE score. Part 3: Top 3 selected with a 2-sentence justification each (why this feature earns its spot in the pre-Diwali launch). Part 4: Bottom 5 dropped with a 1-sentence business reasoning for each drop — not just 'low RICE score' but a strategic reason.
assessmentTask.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM reviewing my work on Day 5: Prioritisation Frameworks — RICE, ICE, and MoSCoW.

I studied today:
- RICE framework: Reach × Impact × Confidence% divided by Effort — each variable defined precisely with the Intercom source
- When to override RICE with strategic judgment, and the three most common RICE scoring mistakes
- MoSCoW as a release scoping tool, distinct from RICE as a portfolio prioritisation tool

Real world context I should apply:
PhonePe APM, September, 8 weeks before Diwali. Engineering capacity for 3 features. Festive season context changes the value of certain features — reach estimates spike, confidence in engagement metrics is higher during festive windows, and time-pressure makes effort estimates critical.

MY TASK TODAY WAS: RICE score 8 PhonePe features, rank them, select top 3 with justification, drop bottom 5 with business reasoning.

Here is my work:
[Pranav pastes his RICE table, ranking, top 3 justification, and drop reasons here]

Review my work and give feedback in EXACTLY this format — do not change these headers:

WHAT I GOT RIGHT:
- [specific bullets with explanation]

WHAT NEEDS CORRECTION:
- [specific bullets with explanation of why — check arithmetic for each feature's score, and whether the top 3 make strategic sense given the Diwali context]

BLIND SPOTS:
- [things I did not consider at all — e.g., strategic fit with Diwali gifting and payments behaviour, the fact that UPI volume spikes 3-4x during festive season, or competitor moves that change the value of specific features]

INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE:
- [one insight about how festive season context in India — Diwali, IPL, Big Billion Day — creates a product prioritisation dynamic unique to Indian consumer internet: Reach estimates change, confidence from past festive data is more reliable, and features that look marginal in a normal month can become critical during a festive window]

OPEN POINTS FOR TOMORROW:
- [1-3 specific, actionable items only]

SCORE: [X/10]
REFORGE COMPETENCY: Feature Specification — [Needs Focus / On Track / Outperform]

days.theme: Product design frameworks — CIRCLES and BUS
days.objective: Produce a complete written CIRCLES answer for a CRED product design case covering all 7 steps, with a specific user definition, India-specific reasoning at each stage, and a clear recommendation in the final summary step.

realWorldAnchor.example: CRED's product design philosophy prioritises aesthetics and reward-loop psychology over feature richness — any design answer for CRED must begin by defining who the user is (the financially responsible, aesthetically oriented, 750+ credit score user) because CRED's design choices are a direct expression of that specific user's mental model and aspirations.

days.teamContext: CIRCLES is most directly relevant to the PM's collaboration with the Design team — it provides a shared language for working through a design decision end-to-end, and prevents the most common failure mode in design reviews: jumping to features without first aligning on users, goals, and success criteria.

tasks.title: The CIRCLES Method: A Product Design Framework — Product School
tasks.summary: This article explains the CIRCLES method — Comprehend the situation, Identify the customer, Report the customer's needs, Cut through prioritisation, List solutions, Evaluate trade-offs, Summarise your recommendation — as the primary framework for answering product design questions in PM interviews and running internal design discussions. This is the core framework for Day 6 and will be used directly in the assessment.
keyTakeaways: CIRCLES is not a rigid checklist — it is a thinking sequence that prevents the two most common PM design failures: jumping to features without defining the user (skipping I and R), and proposing solutions without stating trade-offs (skipping E)

The most important and most skipped step is C — Comprehend the situation: always begin by asking clarifying questions about the goal, constraints, and context before touching users or features; PM interviewers consistently report this as the single biggest differentiator between strong and weak candidates

In Indian PM interviews, CIRCLES requires an additional dimension: at step I (Identify the customer), always check whether your user definition covers both urban English-first users and Tier 2 vernacular users — failure to segment often means your solution works for only the easier half of the Indian market

tasks.title: How to Answer Product Design Questions in PM Interviews — Lenny's Newsletter
tasks.summary: Lenny Rachitsky compiles advice from PM interviewers and hiring managers on what separates strong from weak product design answers — covering not just what to say but how to say it: thinking out loud, handling clarifying questions under pressure, demonstrating user empathy through specificity, and managing time across the 7 CIRCLES steps.
keyTakeaways: The biggest differentiator in design answers is demonstrating user empathy through specificity — 'a 34-year-old CRED user who wants to understand what is pulling her credit score down after adding a new credit card' is more impressive than 'urban working professionals,' because it shows real user thinking

Interviewers consistently score reasoning process higher than the final answer — a wrong recommendation with clear, stepwise logical reasoning scores higher than a correct answer with fuzzy or missing reasoning

Time management guidance for CIRCLES: spend roughly 25% of your time on C/I/R, 20% on Cut, 35% on List and Evaluate, and 20% on Summarise — most candidates rush the user definition and spend too long listing features

tasks.keyword: CIRCLES method CRED credit score feature product design answer PM interview walkthrough India fintech
tasks.whyItMatters: Seeing the CIRCLES framework applied to a product with a niche, well-defined user segment — specifically an Indian fintech with premium positioning and a specific credit score user — prepares Pranav to apply it to the exact CRED case in his assessment. Look for PM interview prep videos or written walkthroughs where a candidate works through CIRCLES on a credit or financial product design question.
whatToLearn: How candidates handle the 'Identify the customer' step when the product has a deliberately narrow user segment — for CRED, the 750+ credit score user is not just a demographic filter but a proxy for specific values (financial discipline, status consciousness, aesthetic sensitivity) that should directly inform every feature suggestion

What a strong CIRCLES summary step sounds like — one paragraph that connects the recommended solution back to the user's primary need, the company's strategic positioning, and 2 key trade-offs that were evaluated and rejected

tasks.promptTitle: Live CIRCLES walkthrough — PM interview simulation for CRED credit score feature
tasks.claudePrompt:
You are a PM interviewer running a live product design interview with me as a candidate for an APM role at CRED.

Today I studied:
- CIRCLES method: 7 steps — Comprehend, Identify, Report, Cut, List, Evaluate, Summarise
- How to answer product design questions: user empathy through specificity, thinking out loud, time management
- CRED's design philosophy: premium aesthetics, reward-loop psychology, exclusively for users with 750+ credit scores

Real world context: This is a live PM interview simulation. I am interviewing for an APM role at CRED in 2024.

Your role today: Play the PM interviewer. When I answer each CIRCLES step, ask the follow-up questions a real interviewer would ask. Push back on vague user definitions. Ask what success looks like before I jump to listing features. If I skip a step, call it out. If my features do not match the user I defined, point out the contradiction.

Case question:
'You are a PM at CRED. Design a feature that helps CRED users understand and improve their credit score. Walk me through your answer.'

We will go step by step through CIRCLES. For each step:
1. I state my answer for that step.
2. You react as the interviewer: ask a follow-up, push back on vague answers, or confirm and invite me to move on.
3. We advance only when each step is sufficiently complete.

After all 7 steps, give feedback in EXACTLY this format:
WHAT WORKED: [bullets]
WHAT MISSED: [bullets]
INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE: [one insight — credit score literacy in India is fundamentally different from the US: CIBIL, Experian, and CRIF operate differently, many high-score users do not know which factors drive their score, and 'improving your credit score' requires financial education that users might find patronising or reassuring depending on how it is framed]
COACHING NOTE: [one specific improvement tip for performing under interview pressure]

Begin with: 'Good. Let's start. Here is your case: You are a PM at CRED. Design a feature that helps CRED users understand and improve their credit score. Take a moment if you need. When you are ready, start with C — Comprehend the situation. What clarifying questions would you ask before diving in?'

bonusTasks.promptTitle: Apply BUS framework to the same CRED case — compare with CIRCLES
bonusTasks.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM helping me compare two product design frameworks: CIRCLES and BUS (Business goal, User goal, Success metrics) using the same CRED case.

I have already completed a full CIRCLES answer for this case: 'Design a feature that helps CRED users understand and improve their credit score.'

Your role today: Guide me through the same case using BUS. BUS is simpler and faster than CIRCLES — it is the framework used in internal design reviews to align a team quickly on what is being built and why, before going into full feature design.

BUS structure:
B — Business goal: What does CRED want to achieve with this feature? Pick the most important one and justify it.
U — User goal: What specific job does the CRED user want done? Write it in JTBD format: 'When [situation], I want to [goal], so I can [outcome].'
S — Success metrics: Name 2-3 metrics that would tell you this feature is working, and 1 guardrail metric that would tell you it is not.

Activity: I answer B, then U, then S. After each answer, you push back or confirm as a senior PM would in a design review.

After both frameworks are complete, ask me: 'What did CIRCLES give you that BUS did not? What did BUS give you that CIRCLES did not? When would you use each in a real product team context?'

Begin with: 'Let's run BUS on the CRED credit score feature. Start with B — Business goal. What is CRED trying to achieve with a credit score education feature? Think about their retention challenges, their monetisation model, and their competitive positioning before you answer.'

assessmentTask.taskDescription: Write a complete, structured CIRCLES answer for the CRED credit score feature design case — all 7 steps written out in full, with a specific user definition, at least 3 feature options evaluated with trade-offs stated, and a clear recommendation in the Summarise step.
assessmentTask.rawMaterial:
Case question (treat this as a written response to a PM interview question):
'You are a PM at CRED. Design a feature that helps CRED users understand and improve their credit score. Walk through your complete answer.'

Context available to you:
- CRED has approximately 12 million members (publicly reported), all with credit scores above 750
- CRED's core product loop: pay credit card bills → earn CRED coins → redeem coins for rewards
- CRED's design philosophy: premium, clean, reward-driven — no clutter, no generic financial advice
- The credit score space in India: CRIF, TransUnion CIBIL, and Experian all operate here — CRED already pulls and shows users their score
- User insight (from CRED's public product communications): many CRED users want to understand WHAT is affecting their score, not just see the number
- Regulatory context: RBI guidelines allow credit bureaus to provide free credit score access — CRED already leverages this, so the score display itself is solved
- One constraint: any feature must fit CRED's premium aesthetic — it cannot look like a generic bank app
assessmentTask.outputFormat: Seven clearly labelled sections, one per CIRCLES step (C / I / R / C / L / E / S). Each section: 3-6 sentences minimum. The Summarise section (S) must be a paragraph of at least 5 sentences that a PM could read aloud as their final recommendation. No bullet-only sections — every section needs at least one sentence of reasoning.
assessmentTask.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM reviewing my written CIRCLES answer for a PM interview design case.

I studied today:
- CIRCLES method: Comprehend, Identify, Report, Cut, List, Evaluate, Summarise — all 7 steps
- How to demonstrate user empathy through specificity rather than category descriptions
- CRED's design philosophy: premium aesthetics, reward-loop psychology, exclusively for 750+ credit score users

Real world context I should apply:
CRED's 12 million members (public figure) all have credit scores above 750. They already see their score in the app. The opportunity is helping them understand and improve it — which requires credit literacy features designed specifically for a financially disciplined, aesthetically conscious, high-engagement user segment.

MY TASK TODAY WAS: Write a complete 7-section CIRCLES answer for 'Design a feature that helps CRED users understand and improve their credit score.'

Here is my work:
[Pranav pastes his full 7-section CIRCLES answer here]

Review my work and give feedback in EXACTLY this format — do not change these headers:

WHAT I GOT RIGHT:
- [specific bullets with explanation]

WHAT NEEDS CORRECTION:
- [specific bullets with explanation of why]

BLIND SPOTS:
- [things I did not consider at all — e.g., gamification of credit improvement milestones fitting CRED's reward loop; regulatory constraints on how credit data can be displayed; the difference between a user who just wants to see their score vs one who wants an action plan; the risk that financial advice features feel patronising to a high-credit-score user who already considers themselves financially disciplined]

INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE:
- [one insight about credit score literacy in India — how CIBIL score awareness differs from the US FICO context, what 'improving your credit score' means differently in India where credit history is shorter for most users, and why many CRED users don't know which specific actions are driving or dragging their score]

OPEN POINTS FOR TOMORROW:
- [1-3 specific, actionable items only]

SCORE: [X/10]
REFORGE COMPETENCY: UX Design Sense — [Needs Focus / On Track / Outperform]

days.theme: Week 1 integration — end-to-end case using all Week 1 tools
days.objective: Apply MECE decomposition, problem framing, RICE prioritisation, and CIRCLES design — in sequence, without prompting — to one end-to-end product case, producing five distinct deliverables that demonstrate Week 1 tools working together.

realWorldAnchor.example: Swiggy Genie — the instant pick-up-and-deliver service for anything, not just food — is believed to have originated from observing that users were already attempting to use Swiggy's food delivery infrastructure for non-food items, prompting a problem reframe from 'more food categories' to 'general urban logistics on demand,' followed by an MVP before scaling.

days.teamContext: Day 7 is a full integration day — all four cross-functional teams are relevant because the end-to-end case requires scoping features (Engineering), designing the solution (Design), defining success metrics (Data), and considering the rollout context (Marketing/Growth).

tasks.title: The Four Big Risks — Marty Cagan, SVPG
tasks.summary: Marty Cagan's framework for product risk — value risk (will users want it?), usability risk (can users use it?), feasibility risk (can engineering build it?), and viability risk (should the business build it?) — provides the integration lens for Day 7. Every tool from Week 1 maps onto one or more of these risks: JTBD and personas address value risk, CIRCLES addresses usability risk, RICE addresses viability risk, and PM-engineering collaboration addresses feasibility. This reading shows how Week 1's toolkit is a coherent risk management system, not a collection of unrelated frameworks.
keyTakeaways: Most product failures happen because one of these four risks was discovered too late — after significant engineering investment, not before it. The PM's job is to reduce all four risks before committing resources, which is why discovery work matters more than execution speed

Value risk and usability risk are addressed through user research and design thinking (JTBD, personas, CIRCLES). Feasibility risk requires close PM-engineering collaboration. Viability risk requires the PM to understand the business model well enough to say no to things that don't move the right metrics

For APMs at Indian consumer internet companies, viability risk is the most commonly underweighted — APMs with strong user empathy sometimes over-index on value and usability while underestimating unit economics constraints specific to Indian markets (logistics costs, payment infrastructure costs, data costs for Tier 2 users)

tasks.promptTitle: Guided warm-up — apply all 5 Week 1 tools to the PayEase KYC case before the full assessment
tasks.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM helping me practice applying all of Week 1's tools in sequence to a single product case, as a warm-up before I do it alone in my assessment.

This week I learned:
- Day 1: The Reforge 12 PM competencies and the PM's core job (valuable, usable, feasible, viable)
- Day 2: MECE structured thinking and first principles reasoning
- Day 3: User personas and Jobs-To-Be-Done
- Day 4: Problem framing (4-component statement) and user interview design
- Day 5: RICE prioritisation framework
- Day 6: CIRCLES product design method
- Day 7 reading: The Four Big Risks as the integration lens

Real world context: I am an APM at PayEase, a fictional Indian fintech offering UPI payments, bill payments, and BNPL. KYC completion rate is 38% against an industry average of 61%. Drop-off is concentrated at the Aadhaar OTP verification step.

Your role today: Guide me through applying each tool in sequence — problem framing first, then MECE, then Cagan risk mapping, then CIRCLES framing. At each step, give me one nudge if I am stuck, but let me do the thinking. The goal is for me to discover the connections between the tools myself, not have them explained to me.

Activity — 4 warm-up steps:
Step 1: I frame the KYC drop-off as a proper 4-component problem statement. You check whether all 4 components are present and precise.
Step 2: I sketch the top-level branches of a MECE tree for the causes of KYC drop-off. You check for MECE validity.
Step 3: I identify which of the 4 Cagan risk categories (value / usability / feasibility / viability) the KYC problem primarily lives in — and which one the solution needs to address most. You push back if I oversimplify.
Step 4: I state which CIRCLES step would be most important to get right for this specific case — and why. You challenge my reasoning.

After all 4 steps, tell me:
- Which tool I am applying most confidently
- Which tool I should slow down on in the full assessment
- One thing to check before I submit my full assessment answer

Begin with: 'Good. Let's start with the foundation. Problem framing first. Using the 4-component structure (user in situation / goal / pain / business impact), frame the KYC drop-off problem at PayEase in one sentence. Go.'

bonusTasks.title: Swiggy Genie product discovery and launch story
bonusTasks.keyword: Swiggy Genie instant delivery launch origin story product discovery non-food India 2020 2021
bonusTasks.summary: Searching for the origin story of Swiggy Genie — the product that extended Swiggy's delivery infrastructure beyond food into general urban pick-up-and-drop — gives Pranav a real end-to-end product discovery story told by an Indian team. Look for product blog posts, PM talks at Indian tech conferences, or inc42/YourStory coverage of the Genie launch that describes the user insight behind the product and how the team decided to scope and build the MVP.

assessmentTask.taskDescription: Given a user research summary, a set of 6 proposed solutions, and a team capacity constraint, produce 5 deliverables in sequence: a scoped problem statement, a 2-level MECE tree with all 6 solutions placed, a RICE-scored prioritisation table with full working, a full CIRCLES answer for the top-ranked solution, and 3 measurable success metrics.
assessmentTask.rawMaterial:
You are an APM at PayEase — a fictional Indian fintech offering UPI payments, bill payments, and a buy-now-pay-later product for online shopping.

Situation:
- 2.4 million users started the KYC verification process in the last 90 days
- Only 38% completed KYC (industry average for similar apps: 61%)
- Drop-off analysis: 54% of incomplete KYC users abandon at Step 3 — Aadhaar OTP verification
- User research (15 user interviews, 200 support tickets): users either do not receive the OTP, receive it late, or do not understand that they have only 3 minutes to enter it before it expires
- Engineering team: 3 engineers, capacity for 4 weeks of work before the quarter ends

Six proposed solutions:

Solution A: Extend OTP expiry from 3 minutes to 10 minutes
Reach: 1,500,000 users | Impact: 2 | Confidence: 85% | Effort: 1 week (1 engineer-week)

Solution B: Add real-time OTP status indicator on screen ('OTP sent — check your SMS inbox')
Reach: 1,500,000 users | Impact: 2 | Confidence: 80% | Effort: 1 week (1 engineer-week)

Solution C: Add DigiLocker as alternative verification path for users with Aadhaar linked
Reach: 600,000 users | Impact: 3 | Confidence: 65% | Effort: 3 weeks (3 engineer-weeks)

Solution D: Send automated WhatsApp recovery message to users who abandon the OTP step
Reach: 900,000 users | Impact: 2 | Confidence: 70% | Effort: 1.5 weeks (1.5 engineer-weeks)

Solution E: Redesign the full KYC flow with a progress bar and plain-language step-by-step instructions
Reach: 2,400,000 users | Impact: 3 | Confidence: 60% | Effort: 4 weeks (4 engineer-weeks)

Solution F: Add live chat support during KYC for users who get stuck
Reach: 300,000 users | Impact: 3 | Confidence: 75% | Effort: 2 weeks (2 engineer-weeks)

Note: For RICE, use Effort in engineer-weeks (as listed above for each solution).
assessmentTask.outputFormat:
Five clearly labelled sections:

SECTION 1 — PROBLEM STATEMENT: One sentence using the format 'When [user in specific situation] tries to [specific goal], they experience [specific pain], which results in [measurable business impact].'

SECTION 2 — MECE TREE: A 2-level indented tree. Top-level branches must be MECE. Each of the 6 solutions (labelled A-F) must appear under exactly one sub-branch — no solution in two branches.

SECTION 3 — RICE TABLE: Full table with columns: Solution | Reach | Impact | Confidence | Effort (eng-weeks) | RICE Score | Working (arithmetic). Followed by a ranking from highest to lowest RICE score.

SECTION 4 — CIRCLES ANSWER: All 7 steps written out for the top-ranked solution from Section 3. The Summarise step must be a full paragraph.

SECTION 5 — SUCCESS METRICS: Three metrics in the format: 'We will know this worked if [measurable outcome] within [time period].' One per bullet.
assessmentTask.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM reviewing my Week 1 integration assessment — a 5-deliverable case on the PayEase KYC drop-off problem.

I studied this week:
- Day 1: Reforge 12 PM competency model — 4 pillars, 12 competencies
- Day 2: MECE structured thinking and first-principles reasoning
- Day 3: User personas and Jobs-To-Be-Done
- Day 4: Problem framing (4-component structure) and user interview design
- Day 5: RICE prioritisation framework with full arithmetic
- Day 6: CIRCLES product design method — all 7 steps
- Day 7 reading: The Four Big Risks as the integration lens

Real world context I should apply:
PayEase KYC drop-off — 62% of users starting KYC abandon at the Aadhaar OTP step. 3 engineers, 4-week capacity, 6 proposed solutions. The core tension: some solutions fix the symptom (OTP timing), others fix the deeper cause (UX clarity), and one attempts an alternative path (DigiLocker).

MY TASK TODAY WAS: Produce 5 deliverables — problem statement, MECE tree with all 6 solutions placed, RICE table with full working, CIRCLES answer for the top-ranked solution, and 3 success metrics.

Here is my work:
[Pranav pastes his full 5-section answer here]

Review my work and give feedback in EXACTLY this format — do not change these headers:

WHAT I GOT RIGHT:
- [specific bullets with explanation]

WHAT NEEDS CORRECTION:
- [specific bullets with explanation of why — check: MECE validity of the tree (no solution in two branches, no branch missing), RICE arithmetic for all 6 solutions, CIRCLES completeness especially steps C and E, and whether success metrics are actually measurable]

BLIND SPOTS:
- [things I did not consider at all — e.g., Aadhaar OTP failure rates vary significantly by telecom operator in India; DigiLocker penetration is real but unevenly distributed by age and geography; RBI has communication guidelines about fintech outreach during KYC that affect Solution D; the 3-minute OTP window is a UIDAI constraint, not a PayEase decision, which affects how Solution A is actually scoped]

INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE:
- [one insight about the Aadhaar-OTP KYC dependency that is specific to India — why this friction point exists, who it hurts most (Tier 2 users, older users, users on slower networks), and how the same drop-off pattern would look completely different in a Western market where phone number verification is the standard]

OPEN POINTS FOR NEXT WEEK:
- [3 specific items from Week 1 that Pranav should revisit in Week 2 — identify his weakest area clearly by name and tool]

WEEK 1 SCORE: [X/10]
REFORGE COMPETENCIES ASSESSED THIS WEEK:
- Feature Specification: [Needs Focus / On Track / Outperform]
- Voice of Customer: [Needs Focus / On Track / Outperform]
- UX Design Sense: [Needs Focus / On Track / Outperform]
- Business Outcome Ownership: [Needs Focus / On Track / Outperform]
